Technical Design Document · v1.0

MultiMind — Multi-AI Collaboration CLI Agent

A command-line multi-agent framework with customizable role orchestration, dynamic topology flattening, unified abstraction of five channel types, group-chat-style collaboration, and quota-aware routing. Zero-config startup, free-tier-first, local fallback that never runs dry.

Status: Design Finalized Date: 2026-07-20 Language: English

1 · Project Overview

MultiMind is a multi-AI collaboration agent that runs in the terminal. It addresses three core needs:

01

Multi-AI Group Chat

Multiple AI roles share the same message bus, supporting @mention for targeted delegation and broadcast, similar to group-chat collaboration.

02

Dual-Channel Access

Supports both API calls and logged-in account access directly through official web/CLI — harvesting account-based free quotas.

03

Free-First

Five types of free sources are unified; quota-aware routing prioritizes free providers, with local models as fallback.

1.1 Design Goals

  • High Flexibility: Role names, responsibilities, counts, and hierarchy depth are all configurable and mutable at runtime.
  • Dynamic Flattening: At any time, all roles can be pulled to the same layer for equal discussion, and the hierarchy can be rebuilt after discussion.
  • Build-It-Right-First-Time: Plugin system, quota pool, failover, MCP tools, sandbox, and checkpoint are built into the first version to avoid costly rework later.
  • Zero-Friction Startup: Works out of the box, can call public-endpoint AI without any configuration; advanced users can progressively add login state and API keys.
  • Portable: Configuration, role orchestration, login state, and quota pool can be imported/exported with one click.

1.2 Non-Goals

  • No GUI client — focus on the CLI experience.
  • No built-in training/fine-tuning capabilities — only inference orchestration.
  • Not a replacement for single-AI coding clients like OpenCode — MultiMind focuses on multi-AI collaborative orchestration.

2 · Core Features Overview

CapabilityDescriptionChapter
Unified Abstraction of Five Channel TypesOfficial CLI reuse / Free API / Web login / Public endpoints / Local fallback; the upper-layer ask() interface is agnostic§4
Customizable Role OrchestrationRole names, responsibilities, counts, and hierarchy depth are configured via TOML, not hardcoded§5
Dynamic Flatten / Rebuild/flatten pulls everyone to the same layer, /rebuild restores the hierarchy; can switch repeatedly within a session§5
GroupChat Message BusAll roles share context, supporting @mention targeting, broadcast, and layer-based message flow§6
Three-Tier Memory SystemShort-term group chat + mid-term stage summaries + long-term project archive; semantic retrieval injection; manual + auto trigger summaries§7
Quota-Aware RoutingFilter candidates by capability tags, score by remaining quota to pick the best, auto-failover on failure§8
One-Click Import/ExportConfig + roles + login state + quota pool packaged as .mmbox for cross-machine migration§9
MCP Tool ProtocolExecutor-layer AI calls search/code execution/file operations via MCP, not just text generation§10
Sandboxed Code ExecutionExecutor runs code in Docker isolation, secure§10
Session CheckpointGroup chat state is serializable, interruptible and resumable, long tasks replayable§10
Zero-Config StartupWorks out of the box with public endpoints; multimind chat requires no configuration§4
TUI GUI-like Terminal InterfaceThree-column layout · arrow-key command selection · group-chat bubbles · powered by Textual§15
Innovation Feature Borrowing & DifferentiationBorrows while-loop core from Grok Build/Claude Code/Aider · three-tier compression · permission modes · Skills/Marketplace · differentiates via multi-AI group chat§16
Auto Git Version ControlExecutor auto-commits on code changes · AI-generated commit messages · lint/test validation chain · rollback on failure · worktree branch isolation§17
Skill Sources & LoadingThree-tier sources (in-house core + open-source generic + LLM-generated long-tail) · dynamic scan priority + config file + model self-judgment · IP risk isolation§18

3 · System Architecture

The system has three layers: the Engine Layer handles role orchestration and group-chat scheduling, the Adapter Layer abstracts the differences of the five channel types, and the Support Layer provides cross-cutting capabilities like quota pool, config, auth, and audit.

3.1 Main Data Flow

User commands enter the Orchestrator via the CLI, which determines role participation based on the current topology: in layered mode, the Leader decomposes tasks and hands them to the Dispatcher for routing to Executors; in flat mode, everyone participates equally in the discussion. Each role utterance goes through the Provider Registry to select a channel and call the AI, with results written back to the group-chat bus, and finally the Leader synthesizes a response to the user.

3.2 Key Decoupling Points

  • Role ≠ Hierarchy: A role instance only records its role_id and current layer; topology is runtime-mutable state.
  • Channel ≠ Model: The same model can be accessed via any channel; routing is specified in the config.
  • Message Bus ≠ Transport: Whether layered or flat, the same group-chat bus is used; switching modes does not lose context.

4 · Five Channel Types Design

All channels implement the unified async ask(prompt, context) -> AsyncIterator[str] interface; the upper layer is completely agnostic to differences. Adding a new AI only requires writing one provider file.

4.1 Channel Type Comparison

ChannelAuthWho PaysReliabilityTypical Provider
① Official CLI ReuseOAuth LoginOfficial free quotaMedium-HighGemini CLI, Codex CLI, Claude Code
② Free APIAPI KeyPlatform free tierMedium-HighGroq, Cerebras, SiliconFlow, OpenRouter :free
③ Web LoginAccount CookiePlatform free conversation quotaMedium (DOM fragile)ChatGPT Web, Claude.ai, DeepSeek, Kimi
④ Public EndpointNonePlatform shared quotaLow (may shut down anytime)glm-5-free, Pollinations, Puter.js
⑤ Local FallbackNoneOwn hardwareHigh (permanent)Ollama, LM Studio, MLX

4.2 Free AI Source Details (Verified Available in 2026)

ChannelProviderFree QuotaRepresentative ModelNotes
① CLIGemini CLI1000 requests/day ProGemini 3.1 ProLogin Google, OAuth[1]
① CLICodex / Claude CodeAccount free quotaGPT / ClaudeReuse their login state
② APIGroq14,400/day (8B), 30 RPMLlama 3.3 70BFastest inference 300 tok/s[2]
② APICerebras1M tokens/dayLlama/Qwen/GPT-OSS8K context limit[3]
② APIGoogle AI Studio1500/day, 1M contextGemini 2.5 FlashPro became paid 4/1[4]
② APISiliconFlow9B and below permanently freeQwen2.5-7B, GLM-4-FlashLow latency in China[5]
② APIOpenRouter :free~27 free modelsDeepSeek R1, Llama 420 RPM / 50 RPD[1]
② APINVIDIA build1000-5000 starter creditsDeepSeek V3.2, Qwen3 Coder 480B~40 RPM[1]
③ WebChatGPT/Claude.ai/Gemini/DeepSeek/Kimi/Qwen/PoeDaily conversation quotaEach flagshipPlaywright scraping
④ Public EndpointOpenCode built-in glm-5-freeRate-limitedGLM-5Zero registration zero Key[6]
④ Public EndpointPollinations30 RPMOpenAI compatibleZero Key[1]
⑤ LocalOllama / LM Studio / MLXUnlimitedQwen3-30B-A3B, Llama 3.3 70BZero-cost fallback[1]
2026 Defunct — Avoid:Google AI Studio free Pro (paid 4/1), Kimi K2.6 OpenRouter :free (lost free tag 6/13), Roo Code (shut down 5/15), SambaNova free tier uncertain.[1]

4.3 Clarification on "Login" vs "Zero-Login"

The user's original "login account via official" requirement solves harvesting account-based quotas (ChatGPT web conversations, Gemini CLI's 1000/day Pro); the fourth type, public endpoints, solves zero-friction fallback — there are models available even when you don't want to register an account. The two are complementary; the router selects by priority: zero-friction first → login quota → local fallback.

The "built-in free models" of clients like OpenCode actually come in three mechanisms: Zen free models (requires OpenCode account login + card binding[7]), built-in zero-config models (truly zero-login[6]), and local Ollama (zero network). OpenCode itself is not a channel type but a client — its "free" offerings fall into our ①②④⑤ four channel types respectively. What MultiMind does is abstract all these underlying channels, then layer multi-AI group-chat orchestration on top.

4.4 Zero-Config Startup Experience

Borrowing OpenCode's out-of-the-box approach, MultiMind also works immediately after first install:

$ pip install multimind
$ multimind chat "hello"          # Answer directly via public endpoint, no config needed
$ multimind login gemini         # Want stronger quota? Login next
$ multimind config set groq.api_key sk-...   # Want to add free API? Configure next
$ multimind group --task "..."   # Want multi-AI group chat? Use directly

Beginners get started with zero friction; advanced users progressively add quota — this neither violates the "login account" requirement nor misses the "out-of-the-box" benefit.

5 · Role Orchestration & Dynamic Topology

5.1 Three-Tier Roles (Example)

Roles are divided into three layers, with any number per layer. Three layers is just an example; two or five layers work too — entirely determined by configuration.

5.2 Dynamic Flatten / Rebuild

Core new capability: at any time, pull everyone to the same layer for equal discussion, then rebuild the hierarchy afterward. Can switch repeatedly within the same session.

5.3 Three Topology Modes

ModeStructureUse Case
layeredFixed layeringExecution-type work with clear, decomposable tasks
flatEveryone same-layer round tableBrainstorming, proposal validation, multi-perspective decision-making
hybridDynamically switchableComplex tasks: layered execution → flatten for key decisions → rebuild to continue

hybrid best fits the user's description: normally work in layers, and when a node requires collective thinking, a single /flatten pulls everyone flat; after discussion, /rebuild returns to continue execution. Can be configured to auto-flatten when "task contains keywords" or "Leader determines multiple opinions are needed", and auto-rebuild after discussion.

5.4 Configuration-Driven (Flexibility Realized)

Roles and topology are entirely driven by TOML config, with hot reload at runtime:

[topology]
mode = "hybrid"            # layered | flat | hybrid
layers = 3                 # Layer depth arbitrary

[[roles]]
id = "leader-1"
name = "Leader"
layer = 1
model = "gemini-3.1-pro"
channel = "cli"            # Via official CLI reuse channel
system_prompt = "files/prompts/leader.md"
count = 1

[[roles]]
id = "dispatcher"
name = "Router"
layer = 2
model = "claude-3.5-sonnet"
channel = "api"
count = 2

[[roles]]
id = "executor"
name = "Executor"
layer = 3
model = "deepseek-v3"
channel = "api"
count = 4

[[links]]                  # Inter-layer connection relationships customizable
from_layer = 1
to_layer = 2
type = "fan-out"           # fan-out | one-to-one | many-to-many

[dynamic]
allow_flatten = true
flatten_trigger = "manual" # manual | auto
rebuild_to = "last"

5.5 Dynamic Switching Commands

multimind group --task "..."                  # Start with configured topology
multimind group --topology flat --task "..."  # Start directly in flat mode
multimind group --roles 5 --task "..."        # Temporarily specify total role count

# During session (interactive):
/flatten                # Pull everyone to same layer for equal discussion
/rebuild                # Rebuild to configured layered topology
/rebuild 2-layer        # Rebuild to specified 2-layer structure
/promote @ExecutorB     # Temporarily promote an AI to host in flat mode
/demote @Leader         # Demote back to regular participant
/add-role researcher --model gemini --layer 2
/remove-role @Router-2

5.6 Design Support

  • Roles decoupled from hierarchy: Each role instance only records role_id and current layer. flatten() sets everyone's layer to 1, rebuild() resets per config — topology is runtime-mutable state, not a hardcoded structure.
  • Message bus unchanged: Whether layered or flat, the same group-chat bus is used. In layered mode messages flow by layer; in flat mode everyone sees broadcasts. Switching modes does not lose context.
  • Role prompt templates externalized + inheritable: Prompts are Markdown files, supporting extends to inherit base roles.

6 · GroupChat Message Bus

All AI roles share the same group-chat context, similar to a group chat. Each message carries {sender, role, content, ts, mentions}, readable by all AIs; @Executor-2 triggers targeted delegation; without @, it's a broadcast.

6.1 Message Flow Sequence

6.2 Message Structure

{
  "msg_id": "uuid",
  "sender": "leader-1",          // Sending role id
  "role": "leader",              // Role type
  "layer": 1,                    // Current layer
  "content": "...",              // Message body
  "ts": 1784516752,
  "mentions": ["executor-2"],    // @'d roles; empty = broadcast
  "in_reply_to": "uuid",         // Reply to a message
  "channel": "cli",              // Which channel produced it
  "provider": "gemini-cli",
  "tokens": {"in": 1200, "out": 350},
  "cost": 0.0                    // Free = 0
}

6.3 Context Management

  • Full visibility: By default, all roles can read the complete group-chat history.
  • Rolling summary: When exceeding a threshold (e.g., 32k tokens), the Leader triggers a rolling summary to compress early messages.
  • Targeted visibility: Sensitive subtasks can be marked visible_to: [role-ids] to restrict visibility.
  • Persistence: All messages are written to SQLite, supporting session replay and resume from breakpoint.

7 · Memory System & Message Flow

The memory system is the sole intermediary connecting the group-chat bus and each role model. All roles do not directly read the group chat or directly call models — each utterance goes through the memory manager to assemble context (recent messages + rolling summary + three-tier memory retrieval), and after the call, writes back to the bus and triggers summary judgment. This way, no matter how many roles run concurrently, memory is the single source of truth.

7.1 Three-Tier Memory Architecture

① Short-Term

Working Memory

Group-chat bus itself + rolling summary. Triggers summary over 32k tok. Session-level lifecycle.

② Mid-Term

Stage Memory

Stage summary + vector index + key facts. Retrievable within project cycle. Semantic recall.

③ Long-Term

Project Memory

Project archive + knowledge graph + lessons learned. Cross-session settling and reuse.

7.2 Daily Summary & Stage Summary

Summaries are the memory's write strategy, at two granularities. Both support auto-trigger and manual trigger.

TypeAuto TriggerManual TriggerAuthorContentStorage
Daily SummaryEvery day 24:00 / Session end/summarize day or multimind summarize dayLeaderToday's tasks/decisions/unresolved/tomorrow's todosMid-term summaries/YYYY-MM-DD.md
Stage SummaryMilestone node / Task completion/summarize stage --id S2 or multimind summarize stageLeader + relevant ExecutorsStage achievement/deliverables/pitfalls/experienceLong-term stages/stage-N.md + lessons store
Manual trigger is a first-class citizen:Users can explicitly request a summary at any time via manual trigger. Whether auto-trigger is enabled or not, /summarize day and /summarize stage can be executed immediately — suitable for solidifying memory at key nodes or as a remedy when auto-scheduling fails. Manual trigger forces execution (ignoring the "too few messages" skip condition) and prints the full summary text for user verification.

Daily Summary Flow (Pseudocode)

async def daily_summary(force=False):
    msgs = bus.fetch(today)
    if not force and token_count(msgs) < 1000:
        return                              # Too few, not worth summarizing
    summary = await leader.ask(
        prompt=DAY_SUMMARY_PROMPT,          # Structured template
        context=msgs
    )
    facts = extract_facts(summary)          # Extract key facts
    await memory.write_mid(
        f"summaries/{today}.md",
        summary, facts=facts, embed=True
    )
    if config.prune_after_summary:
        bus.prune_older_than(today)         # Clean up short-term bus

Stage Summary Flow (Pseudocode)

async def stage_summary(stage_id, force=False):
    daily = memory.fetch_mid(f"summaries/{stage_id}-*")
    key_msgs = bus.fetch_by_tags(stage_id)
    summary = await leader.ask(
        prompt=STAGE_SUMMARY_PROMPT,
        context=daily + key_msgs
    )
    lessons = extract_lessons(summary)      # Extract lessons learned
    graph.update(extract_entities(summary)) # Update knowledge graph
    await memory.write_long(
        f"stages/{stage_id}.md",
        summary, lessons=lessons, graph_updates=True
    )

7.3 Inter-Model Message Flow

7.4 Memory Manager Core Interface

class MemoryManager:
    async def assemble_context(self, role_id, intent):
        """Assemble context before role speaks"""
        ctx = []
        ctx += bus.recent(limit=20)                 # Recent 20 messages
        ctx += bus.rolling_summary()                # Rolling summary
        ctx += await self.retrieve_mid(intent)      # Mid-term semantic retrieval
        ctx += await self.retrieve_long(intent)     # Long-term retrieval
        ctx += self.facts_relevant_to(role_id)      # Facts this role should know
        return self.fit_to_window(ctx, provider_max_tokens)

    async def speak(self, role_id, content):
        """Speak: assemble → call → write back → archive"""
        ctx = await self.assemble_context(role_id, content)
        reply = await provider.ask(role.prompt, ctx)
        msg = bus.write(role_id, reply)
        if self.should_summarize(msg):
            await self.maybe_trigger_summary()      # Auto-trigger judgment
        return reply

    async def summarize_day(self, force=False): ...     # Manual entry
    async def summarize_stage(self, sid, force=False): ...

7.5 Cross-Role Memory Sharing & Isolation

Message TypeVisibilityExample
Broadcast messageAll rolesLeader's task dispatch
@mentionBoth parties onlyDelegating an Executor for private work
Stage summaryAll rolesReadable by all after settling
Lessons learnedAll rolesLong-term shared
Role private memoryThat role onlyA role's intermediate thoughts (optional)

7.6 Long-Context Dynamic Trimming

Different providers have vastly different context windows (Cerebras 8K, Gemini 1M). The memory manager dynamically trims based on the current provider's window size, preserving by priority:

def fit_to_window(ctx, max_tokens):
    if token_count(ctx) <= max_tokens * 0.7:        # Leave 30% for output
        return ctx
    # Priority: Key facts > Recent messages > Long-term retrieval > Rolling summary > Old messages
    ctx = keep_facts(ctx)
    ctx = keep_recent(ctx, limit=10)
    ctx = keep_long_term_top_k(ctx, k=5)
    ctx = keep_summary(ctx)
    return truncate(ctx, max_tokens * 0.7)

8 · Quota-Aware Smart Routing

The router maintains a local quota pool (SQLite), recording each provider's daily limit, remaining, rate, and latency. Tasks arrive with capability tags; candidates are first filtered by channel and capability, then scored by remaining quota to pick the best; on failure or 429, auto-failover to the next provider.

7.1 Capability Tag System

Provider Tags

tags: [long-context, code, reasoning, multimodal, fast, private]

Task Tags

Subtasks declare required capabilities; the router matches candidate providers by tags.

7.2 Routing Priority

Always prioritize consuming free providers, in priority order, with local models as fallback and paid last:

# Routing priority: zero-friction first → login quota → local fallback
[[providers]]
id = "glm-free"
type = "public_endpoint"      # ④ Public endpoint
auth = "none"
priority = 1                  # Highest priority: zero-friction

[[providers]]
id = "gemini-cli"
type = "cli"                  # ① CLI reuse
channel = "oauth"
priority = 10                 # Use login quota after zero-friction exhausted

[[providers]]
id = "groq"
type = "api"                  # ② Free API
priority = 20

[[providers]]
id = "ollama-local"
type = "local"                # ⑤ Local fallback
priority = 99                 # Always fallback

[routing]
strategy = "tag-match-then-quota"
fallback_chain = true
local_as_last_resort = true

7.3 Key Reliability Mechanisms

  • Multi-provider failover: Auto-switch to next provider on call failure/429/timeout, following the priority chain. This is the lifeline for the free stack to "run around the clock"[8].
  • Rate-align to provider's real tier: Set local RPM limit to the provider's real RPM, don't exceed — exceeding turns "in-code skip" into "provider-side 429 counted against quota"[1].
  • Local model fallback layer: After all free APIs are exhausted, auto-fallback to Ollama, ensuring never-unavailable.
  • Cost-aware orchestration: When the Leader assigns subtasks, it considers "whether free providers are sufficient" before using paid ones.

9 · One-Click Import/Export

Configuration, role orchestration, login state, and quota pool can be packaged as a whole for migration — cross-machine, cross-account, backup and restore all use the same mechanism.

8.1 Package Contents

ContentSourceEncryptedDescription
Config file~/.multimind/config.tomlNoprovider, routing, topology, role definitions
Role prompt templates~/.multimind/prompts/*.mdNoRole system prompts, inheritable
Login state~/.multimind/auth/YesCookie, storage_state, OAuth token, API Key
Quota pool snapshotSQLiteNoEach provider's daily usage, remaining
Session historySQLiteOptionalGroup-chat message records, packaged on demand
MetadataGeneratedNoVersion, export time, machine fingerprint, checksum

8.2 Package Format .mmbox

A .mmbox is essentially a ZIP archive with the following agreed structure:

my-multimind.mmbox
├── manifest.json          # Metadata + checksum
├── config.toml            # Main config
├── prompts/               # Role prompt templates
│   ├── leader.md
│   └── executor.md
├── auth.enc               # Encrypted login state (AES-256)
├── quota.db               # Quota pool snapshot (SQLite)
└── sessions/              # Optional: session history
    └── session-xxx.jsonl

manifest.json Example

{
  "format": "mmbox",
  "version": "1.0",
  "exported_at": "2026-07-20T10:30:00Z",
  "machine": "sha256:abc...",     // Source machine fingerprint
  "multimind_version": "0.1.0",
  "contents": {
    "config": "sha256:...",
    "prompts": "sha256:...",
    "auth": "sha256:...",          // Checksum after encryption
    "quota": "sha256:..."
  },
  "sensitive_encrypted": true,
  "encryption": "aes-256-gcm"
}

8.3 CLI Commands

# Export
multimind export                              # Full export to default location
multimind export --output ~/backup.mmbox      # Specify output path
multimind export --include sessions           # Include session history
multimind export --no-auth                    # Exclude sensitive login state (for sharing config)

# Import
multimind import ~/backup.mmbox               # Import and merge
multimind import ~/backup.mmbox --merge       # Merge with existing config (default)
multimind import ~/backup.mmbox --replace     # Fully replace existing config
multimind import ~/backup.mmbox --only auth   # Import only login state
multimind import ~/backup.mmbox --only config # Import only config

# Manage login state vault password (for encryption/decryption)
multimind vault set-password                  # Set/modify vault master password
multimind vault rotate                        # Rotate key

8.4 Security Design

Sensitive data must be encrypted:Login state (Cookie, API Key, OAuth token) is highly sensitive data and must be encrypted with AES-256-GCM on export. The master password is kept locally by the user and not written into .mmbox. The master password is required for decryption on import.
  • Tiered export: --no-auth can export a "pure config package" without login state, suitable for sharing role orchestration and provider config within a team.
  • Selective import: --only auth/config/quota imports only specific parts as needed.
  • Merge strategy: Default --merge, new content overwrites same-name items but keeps others; --replace fully replaces.
  • Checksums: manifest contains sha256 for each file; integrity is verified on import.
  • Cross-machine migration: Login state may be flagged by platform risk control across machines; after import, it's recommended to trigger multimind login --refresh to verify validity.

10 · Build-It-Right-First-Time Checklist

To avoid costly rework later, these capabilities must be built into the first version — retrofitting them later is extremely expensive.

9.1 Architecture & Extensibility

ImprovementWhy It Must Be Built-It-Right-First-Time
Provider pluginizationEach provider implements ask() in one file, auto-registered per spec. Retrofitting the interface later affects all providers
Capability-tag routingProviders carry tags, tasks carry tags. Otherwise, adding multimodal/long-context tasks later requires rewriting the router
Config hot reloadAdding providers/changing routing without restarting the process; file changes take effect immediately
Unified streaming interfaceasync ask() -> AsyncIterator[str]; API SSE and web DOM incremental scraping are unified
Role prompt templates externalized + inheritablePrompts are Markdown, support extends. Externalizing later requires engine changes

9.2 Reliability & Cost

ImprovementWhy It Must Be Built-It-Right-First-Time
Quota pool + usage trackingSQLite records each provider's daily usage/remaining. Adding later loses historical usage
Multi-provider failoverAuto-switch on failure/429/timeout. Lifeline for free stack to run around the clock
Local model fallback layerAuto-fallback to Ollama when free APIs exhausted. Adding later requires rewriting routing priority
Cost-aware orchestrationLeader considers free quota sufficiency when assigning subtasks. Fundamental cost-saving mechanism
Rate-align to real tierLocal RPM set to provider's real limit, avoiding 429 counting against quota
Login state health checkPeriodically probe CLI/web login state, proactively alert on expiry

9.3 Observability & Operations

ImprovementWhy It Must Be Built-It-Right-First-Time
Call audit logEach call records provider/duration/tokens/cost/success. Adding later loses history
multimind stats usage dashboardReal-time view of each provider's usage distribution, remaining quota, success rate
Unified key vaultAll Keys/Cookies centrally encrypted. Migrating keys later is painful

9.4 Features & Ecosystem

ImprovementWhy It Must Be Built-It-Right-First-Time
MCP tool protocolExecutor-layer AI calls tools via MCP. Adding later requires changing the executor ABI
Sandboxed code executionExecutor runs code in Docker isolation. Adding later requires changing the executor architecture
Session checkpoint + resumeGroup-chat state serializable, interruptible and resumable, long tasks replayable
flatten/rebuild as first-classTopology switching is a core capability from the start, not a patch
Three-tier memory system + sole intermediaryMemory manager is the sole intermediary between roles and models, built into v1. Adding later requires changing all role call paths
Summary manual trigger entry/summarize and CLI commands must exist from v1 as a remedy channel for auto-trigger
Vector retrieval + dynamic trimmingMemory semantic retrieval and provider-window-based trimming built into v1, otherwise multi-provider mixing causes context overflow
TUI arrow-key navigation↑↓←→ for command selection/panel switching/message selection built into v1, a core user interaction requirement
Group-chat bubble renderingBubbles colored by role layer + @mention highlighting built into v1, otherwise later rendering changes require rewriting the message stream
TUI and CLI dual modeInteractive scenarios use TUI, pipe/script scenarios fall back to plain text; both paths needed from v1
One-click import/exportConfig/login state/quota pool packaged. Adding later requires changing storage format
Public endpoint auth=none typeAdapter must support unauthenticated channels from v1

9.5 Agent Engine & Security (Research Borrowing)

ImprovementWhy It Must Be Built-It-Right-First-Time
while-loop Agent coreEach role driven by an "observe→think→speak→tool" loop, loops can nest. Changing the execution model later requires rewriting all roles
Typed ToolsAll tools carry schema definitions, MCP tools auto-convert to typed. Adding later requires changing the tool call protocol
Role mode three statesexplore (read-only) / plan (design only) / act (executable) written into role definitions from v1. Adding later requires changing the permission model
Three-tier compression + circuit breakerGroup chat triggers Micro/Auto/Full Compact progressively over threshold, circuit breaker prevents avalanche. Changing later requires rewriting the message bus
Five permission modes + Auto classifiernone/ask/auto/all + risk grading. Adding later requires changing all tool call paths
/cost /privacy transparency commandsExplicitly show spending and data flow. Trust-building must be present from v1
Headless NDJSON streamingNo-TTY outputs NDJSON event stream, for CI orchestration. Adding later requires changing the output layer
Auto Git commitExecutor auto-commits after code changes + lint/test validation, rollback on failure. Adding later requires changing the executor and checkpoint linkage
Skill three-tier sources + IP isolationCore in-house + open-source import + LLM-generated three-tier architecture finalized in v1. Changing source strategy later requires re-auditing all skills
Skill dynamic scan loadingDynamic scan + config file + model self-judgment three mechanisms built into v1. Changing the loading mechanism later requires rewriting all skill registration
SiteAdapter dual-layer architectureBrowserAdapter + SiteAdapter layering built into v1. Changing later requires rewriting all site adapters
SafetyGuard anti-ban protectionHuman-simulation delay + rate limiting + circuit breaker built into v1. Adding later requires changing all site interaction paths
Site TOML config externalizationCSS selectors/modes/safety params via TOML config files. Site redesigns change config not code; must be externalized from v1
Four-tier site extension mechanismTOML config → GenericSiteAdapter fallback → inheritance override → Entry Points plugin. Built into v1; adding later requires changing registry and loading flow
SiteCapability capability declarationEach site declares streaming/file_upload/multi_turn/custom capabilities. Orchestrator uses this for task allocation; built into v1 to avoid changing all adapters later
Config validatorvalidate_profile() covers 12 error scenarios. Built into v1, otherwise bad config only explodes at runtime
Multi-directory config searchEnv var → user dir → built-in dir three-level search, user config overrides built-in. Built into v1 to avoid changing loading paths later
One-click install scriptinstall.sh fully automated install + multimind init config wizard. Must be present from v1 to lower the first-use barrier

11 · CLI Command Design

CommandPurpose
multimind chatSingle-AI conversation (specify model/channel)
multimind group --task "..."Group-chat collaboration, auto-orchestrate roles
multimind group --topology flatStart directly in flat mode
multimind group --roles 5 --task "..."Temporarily specify total role count
multimind login gemini/claude/chatgptBrowser interactive login, persist Cookie/OAuth
multimind login --refreshRefresh existing login state
multimind config set <key> <val>Set config item (e.g., groq.api_key)
multimind config set route.chatgpt channel=browserSpecify which channel a model uses
multimind agents listView connected AIs and channels
multimind agents add --name gpt4 --channel browserDynamically add provider
multimind session list / resume <id>Session management
multimind room watchReal-time watch of group-chat message stream (Rich rendering)
multimind statsUsage dashboard (each provider's usage/remaining/success rate)
multimind summarize dayManually trigger daily summary (ignores auto-skip conditions)
multimind summarize stage --id S2Manually trigger stage summary
multimind memory list / search "keyword"List/semantically search memory entries
multimind memory show <id>View details of a memory entry
multimind memory forget <id>Delete a memory entry (GDPR-friendly)
multimind memory export --range 2026-07Export memory for a month
/summarize day / /summarize stageManually trigger summary in session
multimind export [--output path] [--include sessions] [--no-auth]Export .mmbox
multimind import <path> [--merge|--replace] [--only auth|config|quota]Import .mmbox
multimind vault set-password / rotateManage encryption master password
/flatten / /rebuildDynamically flatten/rebuild topology in session
/promote @x / /demote @yTemporarily promote/demote roles in flat mode
/add-role / /remove-roleDynamically add/remove roles in session

12 · Project Structure & Tech Stack

11.1 Directory Structure

multimind/
├── pyproject.toml
├── src/multimind/
│   ├── cli.py                  # Typer entry
│   ├── config.py               # Account/Key/routing config · hot reload
│   ├── engine/
│   │   ├── orchestrator.py     # Role orchestration engine
│   │   ├── roles.py            # Leader/Dispatcher/Executor definitions
│   │   ├── groupchat.py        # GroupChat message bus (@mention/broadcast)
│   │   └── topology.py         # Layered/flat/dynamic switch
│   ├── adapters/
│   │   ├── base.py             # AIAdapter abstract: async ask()
│   │   ├── cli_reuse.py        # ① Official CLI reuse channel
│   │   ├── api_client.py       # ② Free API channel (httpx)
│   │   ├── browser.py          # ③ Web login channel (Playwright)
│   │   ├── public_endpoint.py  # ④ Public endpoint channel (zero auth)
│   │   ├── local.py            # ⑤ Local fallback channel (Ollama)
│   │   └── registry.py         # Model → channel routing
│   ├── providers/              # Each AI's web/API selectors and endpoints
│   │   ├── chatgpt.py  claude.py  gemini.py  deepseek.py  glm.py
│   ├── auth/
│   │   ├── session.py          # Cookie/storage_state persistence
│   │   ├── login.py            # Interactive login flow
│   │   └── vault.py            # Encrypted vault
│   ├── routing/
│   │   ├── quota.py            # Quota pool · SQLite
│   │   ├── tags.py             # Capability tag matching
│   │   └── failover.py         # Failover chain
│   ├── memory/                 # Memory system
│   │   ├── manager.py          # Memory manager (sole intermediary)
│   │   ├── short_term.py       # Short-term · group-chat bus + rolling summary
│   │   ├── mid_term.py         # Mid-term · stage summary + vector retrieval
│   │   ├── long_term.py        # Long-term · project archive + knowledge graph
│   │   ├── summary.py          # Daily/stage summary (manual+auto)
│   │   └── retrieve.py         # Retrieval injection + dynamic trimming
│   ├── tools/
│   │   ├── mcp.py              # MCP tool protocol
│   │   └── sandbox.py          # Docker sandbox
│   ├── git/
│   │   ├── auto_commit.py      # Auto-commit engine
│   │   ├── worktree.py         # worktree isolation management
│   │   ├── lint_chain.py       # lint/test validation chain
│   │   └── checkpoint_link.py  # Commit and checkpoint linkage
│   ├── io/
│   │   ├── export.py           # .mmbox export
│   │   ├── import_.py          # .mmbox import
│   │   └── manifest.py         # Package metadata
│   ├── session/
│   │   ├── checkpoint.py       # Session serialization/resume
│   │   └── replay.py           # Long task replay
│   └── ui/
│       ├── render.py            # Rich group-chat rendering (non-TUI scenarios)
│       └── tui/                 # Textual TUI app
│           ├── app.py           # TUI main app · three-column layout
│           ├── widgets/
│           │   ├── command_tree.py  # Left column command nav tree
│           │   ├── chat_view.py     # Middle column group-chat box
│           │   ├── status_panel.py  # Right column status panel
│           │   ├── input_box.py     # Input box + @mention/command menu
│           │   └── round_table.py   # Round table view (after flatten)
│           ├── views/               # Sub-views
│           │   ├── summary_view.py
│           │   ├── login_view.py
│           │   ├── stats_view.py
│           │   ├── memory_view.py
│           │   └── io_view.py
│           └── styles.tcss          # Textual CSS styles
└── tests/

11.2 Tech Stack Selection

LayerSelectionRationale
LanguagePython 3.11+Ecosystem, asyncio, AI-friendly
CLI FrameworkTyper + RichType safety + nice terminal group-chat view
TUI FrameworkTextualGUI-like three-column layout · arrow-key nav · CSS styles · Python native
Browser AutomationPlaywrightLogin state persistence, stable selectors, supports streaming scraping
API Clienthttpx (async)Unified async, streaming SSE
Role OrchestrationIn-house Orchestrator + system prompt injectionRoles are prompt templates
Message BusIn-memory queue + SQLite persistenceGroup-chat context replayable
Memory SystemSQLite + sqlite-vss + local embedding modelThree-tier memory · semantic retrieval · single source of truth
Quota Pool/AuditSQLiteLightweight, zero-dependency, serializable
ConfigTOML (~/.multimind/config.toml)Account, Key, routing rules
Encryptioncryptography (AES-256-GCM)Login state vault
SandboxDocker SDK for PythonCode execution isolation
Local InferenceOllama HTTP APIZero-config local models

13 · Configuration Spec

Complete TOML example covering five channel types, role orchestration, dynamic topology, and routing strategy:

# ~/.multimind/config.toml

[topology]
mode = "hybrid"                # layered | flat | hybrid
layers = 3
default_role_count = 6

[[roles]]
id = "leader-1"
name = "Leader"
layer = 1
model = "gemini-3.1-pro"
channel = "cli"
system_prompt = "files/prompts/leader.md"
count = 1

[[roles]]
id = "dispatcher"
name = "Router"
layer = 2
model = "claude-3.5-sonnet"
channel = "api"
count = 2

[[roles]]
id = "executor"
name = "Executor"
layer = 3
model = "deepseek-v3"
channel = "api"
count = 4

[[links]]
from_layer = 1
to_layer = 2
type = "fan-out"

[dynamic]
allow_flatten = true
flatten_trigger = "manual"     # manual | auto
rebuild_to = "last"

# ===== Five channel type providers =====

[[providers]]
id = "glm-free"
type = "public_endpoint"       # ④ Public endpoint
base_url = "https://open.bigmodel.cn/api/paas/v4"
model = "glm-5-flash-free"
tags = ["fast", "code"]
auth = "none"
quota = { per_minute = 20 }
priority = 1

[[providers]]
id = "pollinations"
type = "public_endpoint"
base_url = "https://text.pollinations.ai/openai"
model = "openai"
tags = ["fast", "general"]
auth = "none"
quota = { per_minute = 30 }
priority = 2

[[providers]]
id = "gemini-cli"
type = "cli"                   # ① CLI reuse
channel = "oauth"
model = "gemini-3.1-pro"
tags = ["long-context", "multimodal", "reasoning"]
quota = { per_day = 1000, per_minute = 30 }
priority = 10

[[providers]]
id = "groq"
type = "api"                   # ② Free API
base_url = "https://api.groq.com/openai/v1"
model = "llama-3.3-70b"
tags = ["fast", "code"]
quota = { per_day = 14400, per_minute = 30, tokens_per_minute = 6000 }
priority = 20

[[providers]]
id = "cerebras"
type = "api"
model = "qwen3-32b"
tags = ["fast", "long-context"]
quota = { per_day_tokens = 1000000, per_minute = 30 }
priority = 30

[[providers]]
id = "siliconflow-free"
type = "api"
model = "qwen2.5-7b"
tags = ["fast", "code"]
quota = { per_day = "unlimited" }
priority = 40

[[providers]]
id = "ollama-local"
type = "local"                 # ⑤ Local fallback
model = "qwen3:30b-a3b"
tags = ["code", "reasoning", "private"]
quota = { per_day = "unlimited" }
priority = 99

[routing]
strategy = "tag-match-then-quota"
fallback_chain = true
local_as_last_resort = true

[io]
export_format = "mmbox"
encrypt_auth = true
default_export_path = "~/.multimind/exports"

[memory]
enabled = true
backend = "sqlite"                    # sqlite | postgres
vector_index = "sqlite-vss"           # Vector index
embed_model = "local"                 # Local embedding model

[memory.short_term]
bus_retention = "7d"                  # Group-chat bus retained 7 days
rolling_summary_threshold = 32000     # Trigger rolling summary over 32k tok
recent_messages_in_context = 20       # Inject recent 20 messages

[memory.mid_term]
daily_summary_schedule = "0 0 * * *"  # Auto-trigger daily at 24:00
daily_summary_model = "leader-1"      # Author role
allow_manual = true                   # Allow manual trigger (default on)
stage_summary_on_milestone = true     # Auto-trigger at milestones

[memory.long_term]
auto_extract_facts = true             # Auto-extract key facts
auto_update_graph = true              # Auto-update knowledge graph
lessons_enabled = true                # Lessons learned store

[memory.retrieval]
top_k_mid = 5                         # Mid-term memory retrieval top 5
top_k_long = 3                        # Long-term memory retrieval top 3
similarity_threshold = 0.75           # Similarity threshold
rerank = true                         # Re-ranking
fit_to_provider_window = true         # Dynamic trim by provider window

[vault]
algorithm = "aes-256-gcm"

14 · Challenges & Countermeasures

ChallengeCountermeasure
Web DOM selectors fragileCentrally maintain selector config in provider, data-testid priority + text fallback matching
Login state expiry / CAPTCHAstorage_state persistence + auto-fallback to interactive login on failure; pause and prompt user when CAPTCHA detected
Web streaming output scrapingListen to DOM mutation events for incremental concatenation, not polling
Multi-AI context explosionGroup-chat bus does summary compression; Leader triggers rolling summary over threshold
Anti-scraping rate limitsAdapter built-in backoff retry + channel degradation (auto-switch to API when browser limited)
Free providers may shut down anytimeQuota pool daily health probe, auto-remove from routing candidates on failure, community-maintained provider list
Cross-machine login state risk controlTrigger login --refresh after import to verify; prompt re-login on failure
Multi-role concurrent provider rate limitsAdapter built-in token bucket, globally aggregate each provider's RPM, avoid roles squeezing each other's quota
Group-chat message consistencyAll messages serially written to SQLite via bus, roles read based on snapshots, avoiding concurrency races
Sandbox securityExecutor code runs in Docker container, restricts network/filesystem/CPU/memory, auto-kill on timeout
Config migration compatibility.mmbox carries version field, import runs migration functions by version
Next step:Design is finalized here. Can proceed to implementation phase — recommend first building a runnable skeleton (with one public-endpoint provider + one free API provider + quota pool core + flatten/rebuild logic + export/import), verify the main chain works, then fill in other channels and roles.

15 · TUI Terminal Interface Design

Pure command-line operation is cumbersome; MultiMind provides a GUI-like TUI (Terminal User Interface) — three-column layout, arrow-key command navigation, group chat presented as chat-box bubbles. Built on the Textual framework, Python-native, seamlessly integrated with the engine.

15.1 Overall Layout: Three Columns + Top/Bottom Bars

AreaContentInteraction
Top BarTitle · Mode (hybrid/layered/flat) · Role count · Connection statusFixed display
Left ColumnCommand nav tree (session/config/memory/migration groups)↑↓ select, Enter execute, → expand sub-items
Middle ColumnGroup-chat box (bubbles) + input boxMain interaction area
Right ColumnRole/quota/provider usage/topology statusReal-time refresh
Bottom BarShortcut hintsFixed display

15.2 Arrow-Key Navigation (Core Interaction)

The user explicitly requested arrow keys for selecting commands and command content; all TUI navigation is designed around arrow keys:

Left Column Command Tree

↑↓ up/down to select commands, Enter execute, → expand sub-items, ← collapse.

/ Command Menu

Type / to pop up a three-segment list (command completion / recent tasks / command history), ↑↓ select, letters filter in real-time, Enter execute.

Panel Focus Switching

←→ switch focus between left/middle/right columns, focus border highlighted.

Chat History

When input box is empty, ↑↓ browse history messages (like shell history), Tab complete commands/role names/paths.

15.3 Group-Chat Box (Bubble Form)

The group chat does not use a plain text stream, but rather bubble form like IM apps, color-coded by role layer:

Message TypeStyleAlignmentLabel
Leader speechPurple background · left purple color barLeft-alignedL1 tag
Dispatcher speechCyan background · left cyan color barLeft-alignedL2 tag
Executor speechDark background · left green color barRight-alignedL3 tag
System messageAmber dashed border · centeredCenteredDispatch/status change notifications
@mentionPurple background capsule highlightInlineMentioned role receives notification
  • Avatar color bar: Each bubble has a 2px color bar on the left identifying the role layer.
  • Timestamp: 9px gray time floating right at bubble header.
  • "Typing" animation: Three bouncing dots, knowing which role is thinking.
  • Code in messages: Monospace font + cyan highlight, inline rendering.
  • Date separator: Centered gray capsule separator across days.

15.4 Input Box Interaction

❯ @Leader I'm done here, ready to synthesize█
@ mention role · / command · ↑↓ history messages    Shift+Enter newline · Enter send
  • @ mention: Type @ to pop up role list, ↑↓ select, Enter insert.
  • / command: Type / to pop up command menu (see 15.2).
  • Shift+Enter newline, Enter send.
  • Tab complete: Commands, role names, file paths.

15.5 Shortcut Key Table

ShortcutFunctionContext
↑ / ↓Up/down selectLeft command tree / / menu / @ list / input box history
← / →Switch panel focus / expand·collapseLeft↔Middle↔Right / command tree sub-items
EnterExecute / SendCommand tree execute / menu select / input box send
Shift+EnterNewlineInput box
TabCompleteCommand / role name / path
/Trigger command menuInput box
@Trigger role mentionInput box
EscClose menu / BackAny popup menu
Ctrl+PPause/resume group chatGlobal
Ctrl+LClear screenMiddle column chat box
Ctrl+SManually trigger daily summaryGlobal (see §7.2)
Ctrl+GSwitch topology (flatten/rebuild)Global (see §5.2)
F1HelpGlobal
Ctrl+QQuitGlobal

15.6 Tech Selection: Textual

OptionProsConsConclusion
Textual (Recommended)Python-native, CSS styles, rich components, responsive layout, native arrow-key supportPython ecosystem onlyAdopted
Rich + In-houseLightweight, existing foundationAll interaction logic must be self-writtenNot adopted
prompt_toolkitMature input handlingWeak layout capabilityNot adopted

Reason for choosing Textual: It natively supports CSS styles, arrow-key navigation, multi-panel layout, and message stream scrolling — almost tailor-made for this TUI. MultiMind itself is Python, seamlessly integrated. Rich continues to be used as Textual's underlying rendering library for non-TUI scenarios (e.g., plain command output for multimind stats).

15.7 TUI Sub-Views

Besides the main interface, the TUI also provides several dedicated sub-views, all accessible via the left command tree:

Sub-ViewTriggerContent
Round Table ViewAuto-switch after /flattenAll roles arranged in a same-layer circle, lines show the pair currently in conversation
Summary View/summarize day|stageStructured display of full summary, supports arrow-key scrolling, e edit
Login Flowmultimind login <provider>Embedded browser step guidance, real-time login progress
Usage DashboardLeft column statsEach provider's usage bar chart, remaining quota, success rate
Memory BrowseLeft column memoryThree-tier memory tree browse + semantic search box
Import/ExportLeft column export/importWizard-style packaging/restore, with sensitive data encryption prompts

15.8 Responsive & Degradation

  • Narrow terminal degradation: Width < 100 columns hides right column, < 70 columns collapses left column to top dropdown.
  • Non-interactive mode: Pipe/redirection scenarios (multimind chat | jq) auto-fallback to plain text output, no TUI.
  • No-TTY degradation: CI/script scenarios output plain JSON line stream, maintaining programmability.
  • Theme: Default dark, --theme light switches to light, follows terminal color scheme.

16 · Innovation Feature Borrowing & Differentiation

This chapter summarizes research findings on three categories of cutting-edge CLI Agents — Grok Build (xAI, open-sourced 2026-07), Claude Code leaked source (two batches in 2025-02 and 2026-03), and mainstream open-source projects like OpenCode / Aider / Cline / Continue.dev / OpenHands / Cursor CLI / Windsurf — distilling innovative features that can be incorporated into MultiMind, and clarifying MultiMind's differentiation positioning to avoid reinventing the wheel.

16.1 Research Subjects Overview

ProjectTypeCore InnovationsImplications for MultiMind
Grok BuildxAI open-source CLI (Apache 2.0, Rust)Multi-agent parallel · tool-porting adapter · Skills/Plugins/Marketplace · Hooks lifecycle · Plan/Ask/Code three states · inline Mermaid terminal rendering · ACP protocol · headless streaming JSONParallel orchestration and extension ecosystem template
Claude Code (leaked source)Anthropic closed-source CLI (~510k lines TS)while-loop Agent core · typed tools · three-tier compression + circuit breaker · KAIROS guardian · AutoDream memory consolidation · sub-agent three states · Fork/Teammate/Worktree · five permission modes + Auto classifier · 23-layer Bash security check · fake tools slowdown · frustration regexDeep reference for memory and security systems
OpenCodeOpen-source CLI (Go/TS)Client/Server architecture · models.dev registry · LSP in-loop · 6 built-in agents · declarative permissions · compaction agent · git worktree parallelArchitecture layering and model registry
AiderOpen-source CLI (Python)Fine-grained auto-commit · Repo Map (tree-sitter) · multiple edit formats · Architect Mode · watch files · polyglot benchmarkCode map and commit granularity
ClineVSCode extensionPlan/Act separation · step-by-step diff approval · checkpoint timeline · real-time token dashboard · MCP · .clinerulesApproval flow and cost visualization
Continue.devVSCode/JetBrains extensionContext Providers architecture · MCP universal data interface · full IDE coverageContext provider abstraction
OpenHandsOpen-source Agent platformDocker sandbox runtime · three-tier image tags · headless mode · runtime plugins · named volumes + overlaySandbox and runtime isolation
Cursor CLI / WindsurfCommercial CLI/IDECloud handoff · Plan/Ask · one-click MCP auth · Background Agents · Flows · Memory+Rules · TurboCloud collaboration and rules system

16.2 Borrowed Feature Panorama Matrix

The table below summarizes by four columns: "Feature · Source · MultiMind Adoption Approach · Priority". P0 is required for v1, P1 is optional/simplified for v1, P2 is for later iterations.

FeatureSourceMultiMind Adoption ApproachPriority
while-loop Agent coreClaude CodeEach group-chat round driven by "observe→think→speak→tool" loop, role loops can nestP0
Typed ToolsClaude CodeAll tools defined as typed functions with schema, MCP tools auto-convert to typedP0
Coordinator + Subagents parallelGrok Build / Claude CodeLeader is Coordinator, Dispatcher dispatches subtasks to Executors for parallel executionP0
Sub-agent three states (Explore/Plan/General)Claude CodeRole mode field: explore (read-only) / plan (design only) / act (executable)P0
git worktree parallel executionOpenCode / AiderMultiple Executors each occupy independent worktree, parallel code changes without conflict, merge on completionP1
Plan/Ask/Code three statesGrok BuildSession-level /mode plan|ask|code, plan disables execution, ask only Q&A, code fully openP0
Three-tier compression + circuit breakerClaude CodeGroup chat over threshold triggers MicroCompact→AutoCompact→FullCompact, with circuit breaker preventing avalancheP0
AutoDream memory consolidationClaude CodeIdle background organizes mid-term memory, dedup, extract facts, update knowledge graphP1
Compaction AgentOpenCodeDedicated role for compression, non-blocking main chainP1
Prompt Caching boundariesClaude CodeCache system prompt + role settings by provider capability; group-chat incremental part doesn't break cacheP1
Repo Map (tree-sitter)AiderCodebase structure summary as shared context injected to all rolesP1
Three-tier memory indexClaude CodeShort-term rolling + mid-term vector + long-term graph, aligned with §7 memory systemP0
Five permission modes + Auto classifierClaude CodeRole permissions: none/ask/auto/all, Auto mode classifies by command riskP0
23-layer Bash security checkClaude CodeMulti-layer validation before command execution (path/network/delete/privilege escalation etc.)P1
Sandbox runtime (Docker)OpenHands / Grok BuildExecutor code execution isolated, named volumes for persistenceP0
Session CheckpointGrok Build / Cline / Claude CodeGroup-chat state snapshots, support rollback to any decision point (already listed in §10)P0
Real-time Token/cost dashboardClineTUI status bar real-time display of each role's consumption, remaining quotaP0
/cost /privacy transparency commandsGrok BuildExplicitly show spending and data flow, enhance trustP0
Frustration RegexClaude CodeDetect user/role repeated failure signals, trigger degradation or role switchP1
Fake Tools slowdownClaude CodeInsert confirmation "fake tools" before high-risk actions, force manual second confirmationP2
Skills/Plugins/Marketplace three-tier extensionGrok BuildSkill (single file) → Plugin (package) → Marketplace (distribution), aligned with §10 plugin systemP1
MCP universal tool protocolCline / Continue.devAlready listed as §10 core, all external tools go through MCP uniformlyP0
Context Providers abstractionContinue.devFile/Git/Terminal/Web/Memory unified as ContextProvider, injected on demandP1
Tool-Porting AdapterGrok BuildWrap Codex/OpenCode-style tools as unified interface, reuse ecosystemP1
Hooks lifecycleGrok Buildpre-edit/post-edit/pre-command/post-command/on-error hooksP1
Headless streaming JSONGrok Build / OpenHandsNo-TTY mode outputs NDJSON, for orchestration and CI integrationP0
Inline Mermaid terminal renderingGrok BuildWhen role outputs Mermaid, TUI renders inline as ASCII/Unicode diagramP1
CLAUDE.md/AGENTS.md compatibilityGrok BuildRead project-level rule files, auto-inject role settingsP1
Fine-grained auto-commit + lint/testAiderExecutor can auto-commit each step and run validation, rollback on failureP1
Multiple edit formatsAiderdiff/whole/udiff/search-replace multiple code-change methods to suit different modelsP1
Cloud handoff / Background AgentsCursor CLILong tasks hand off to cloud execution, release local (v2 consideration)P2
Benchmark leaderboardAiderBuilt-in model capability benchmark, assists routing decisionsP2

16.3 Agent Engine & Parallel Orchestration

while-loop core: Borrowing from Claude Code, each role's single-round interaction is essentially a while not done loop — observe input, think, choose tool or speak, execute, observe again. The group-chat bus chains multiple role loops: Leader loop drives Dispatcher loop drives Executor loop, forming a nestable loop stack.

Coordinator + Subagents parallel: Grok Build's coordinator + sub-agent model naturally fits MultiMind's Leader/Dispatcher/Executor. When the Dispatcher dispatches multiple independent subtasks, it can launch multiple Executor sub-loops in parallel; results aggregate and the Dispatcher summarizes and reports to the Leader.

Sub-agent three states: Claude Code's Explore/Plan/General three states map to the role mode field. explore mode roles are read-only (suitable for research tasks), plan mode only produces plans without execution (suitable for Leader/Dispatcher), act mode can call tools to execute (suitable for Executor). This is orthogonal to the session-level /mode plan|ask|code (borrowed from Grok Build): session-level mode constrains everyone, role-level mode constrains individuals.

git worktree parallel: When multiple Executors modify the same repo, borrowing from OpenCode/Aider, each Executor gets an independent git worktree, non-interfering; on completion, the Dispatcher leads the merge. This is especially critical for the "flatten and multiple people change code simultaneously" scenario.

16.4 Context Compression & Memory Enhancement

Three-tier compression + circuit breaker: Claude Code's MicroCompact (light summary) / AutoCompact (structured compression) / FullCompact (full rebuild) three-tier mechanism is directly used for the group-chat bus. When group-chat message count or tokens exceed threshold, trigger progressively; the circuit breaker detects consecutive compression failures and stops compression with an alert, preventing avalanche. This connects with §7's short-term memory rolling summary.

AutoDream memory consolidation: Borrowing Claude Code's "sleep" memory organization, MultiMind triggers AutoDream in the background when idle (no user input for N seconds): dedup mid-term memory, extract entity relationships to update the knowledge graph, evict low-value entries. Manual /summarize is the explicit entry to AutoDream.

Repo Map shared context: Aider's tree-sitter code map serves as shared context for all roles, avoiding each role repeatedly scanning the codebase. Executors incrementally update the Repo Map and broadcast after code changes.

Prompt Caching boundaries: By provider capability (e.g., Anthropic, Gemini support prompt caching), the system prompt + role settings + group-chat history prefix form a cacheable segment; incremental messages go at the end without breaking cache, reducing multi-role polling cost.

16.5 Security, Permissions & Observability

Five permission modes: Claude Code's none/ask/auto/all map to role permissions. Leader defaults to ask (confirm before execution), Dispatcher defaults to auto (auto for low-risk, ask for high-risk), Executor defaults to auto. /mode can switch temporarily. The Auto classifier borrows its risk grading (path/network/delete/privilege escalation dimensions).

Sandbox + multi-layer command validation: Executor code execution in Docker sandbox (borrowing OpenHands named volumes + overlay); before command execution, Claude Code-style multi-layer validation chain, high-risk commands force manual confirmation.

Checkpoint timeline: Borrowing Cline's checkpoint timeline, group-chat key decision points auto-snapshot each round; TUI can visually roll back to any node.

Real-time cost dashboard: TUI status bar borrows Cline's real-time display of each role's token consumption, remaining quota, current provider; /cost details, /privacy shows data flow (borrowing Grok Build's transparency commands).

Frustration detection and fake tools: Claude Code's Frustration Regex detects consecutive role failures (e.g., Executor repeatedly errors), triggering role switch or degradation; fake tools insert forced confirmation steps before high-risk actions as "cognitive speed bumps". MultiMind adopts the former (P1), the latter as a P2 option.

16.6 Tool Ecosystem & Extension Mechanism

MCP universal protocol: All external tools (search/code execution/file/web) go through MCP uniformly, consistent with Cline/Continue.dev. MCP tools auto-convert to typed tools for role invocation.

Skills/Plugins/Marketplace three tiers: Borrowing from Grok Build — skills are single-file prompt/script (quick reuse), plugins are packaged skill+tool+config (distributable), marketplace is plugin index (community sharing). Merged with §10 plugin system.

Context Providers: Borrowing from Continue.dev, unify file/Git/terminal/web/memory as ContextProvider interface; roles declare which contexts to inject on demand, avoiding bulk insertion.

Tool-Porting Adapter: Borrowing from Grok Build, wrap Codex/OpenCode/Aider-style tools as MultiMind's unified interface, directly reusing existing ecosystem tools.

Hooks lifecycle: Borrowing Grok Build's pre-edit/post-edit/pre-command/post-command/on-error hooks; plugins can attach to group-chat key events (e.g., "before Executor speaks", "after Leader decides").

Headless streaming JSON: No-TTY scenarios output NDJSON (borrowing Grok Build/OpenHands), for CI orchestration and external program consumption of group-chat event streams.

16.7 MultiMind Differentiation Positioning

The above projects are mostly "single Agent + tool calls" or "single Agent + sub-agent parallel" models. MultiMind's core difference is multi-AI native group-chat collaboration — not a single brain decomposing tasks, but multiple independent AI instances conversing equally in group-chat form, flattenable and layerable. The table below compares the differentiation points:

DimensionMainstream CLI (Claude Code/Grok Build/Aider etc.)MultiMind
Collaboration modelSingle main Agent + sub-agents (master-slave)Multi-AI group chat (peer + layered switchable)
AI sourceSingle provider or fixed fewFive channel types (CLI/API/Web/public/local), mixed scheduling
Free capabilitySome built-in free modelsQuota-aware routing, auto-exhaust free quota before degrading
TopologyFixed master-slaveDynamic flatten/rebuild, switch layers within session
MemorySingle Agent single memoryGroup-chat shared + role-isolated three-tier memory, sole intermediary
InteractionPure CLI or IDE-embeddedTUI GUI-like (arrow keys + bubble group chat) + Headless dual mode
ExtensionPlugins/MCPSkills/Plugins/Marketplace + MCP + tool-porting adapter
Import/ExportConfig files.mmbox one-click package (config + login state + quota pool + memory)

Conclusion: MultiMind does not reimplement Claude Code's single-brain security and compression, or Grok Build's parallel core, but uses these as the single-role capability foundation, building a multi-AI group-chat collaboration layer on top — this is the gap in the current open-source ecosystem.

17 · Auto Git & Version Control

Borrowing Aider's fine-grained auto-commit philosophy, MultiMind auto-triggers Git commits after the Executor modifies code, combined with a lint/test validation chain and checkpoint linkage, achieving a "change code = commit, commit = validate, failure = rollback" safety net. This chapter defines Auto Git's trigger timing, commit strategy, branch model, validation chain, and config options.

17.1 Trigger Timing

Auto-commit triggers at the following timings, implemented by the AutoGit module listening to group-chat bus events:

Trigger PointDescriptionDefault Switch
After Executor single-step code changeAfter Executor calls edit/write tools to modify files, commits before that round loop ends. Finest granularityOn (configurable to stage-end)
Dispatcher stage completionAfter all Executors for a subtask assignment complete, Dispatcher triggers one commit when summarizingOn
Leader decision landedAfter Leader produces final plan and user confirms, commits one milestone snapshotOn
Manual /commitUser or any role triggers explicit commit at any time, not subject to auto policyAlways available
Session suspend / exitAuto-commits unsaved changes on session /suspend or exit, preventing lossOn

17.2 Commit Granularity & Strategy

Logical change granularity: Not file-granularity, but "one logical change" granularity. All file changes in an Executor's single-step loop (possibly cross-file) are grouped as one commit; the commit message is generated by that Executor role, describing the change intent.

AI-generated commit message: Before Executor commits, it calls its own capability to generate a Conventional Commits-compliant message: type(scope): description. Dispatcher stage commits use a summary message generated by the Dispatcher (feat: complete XXX subtask). Leader milestone commits carry the [milestone] tag.

Pre-commit staging: Only files touched by the Executor's current round are git added, not accidentally staging user's manually modified untracked files. Precise staging via git add <specific files>.

Empty commit protection: No empty commits generated when there are no changes, avoiding polluting history.

17.3 Branch Model & worktree Isolation

Main branch protection: Auto-commits do not directly write to main/master by default, but on working branches:

ScenarioBranch StrategyMerge Method
Single Executor serialWorking branch mm/<session-id>, auto-commits heregit merge --ff back to main after user confirms
Multiple Executors parallel (worktree)Each Executor independent worktree + branch mm/<session-id>/<executor>Dispatcher leads sequential merges to mm/<session-id>, conflicts revert to user
User explicit /commit mainDirect commit to main branchNo merge, direct commit
Flat mode group-chat code changeAll roles share working branch, commit in speaking orderNo merge

worktree lifecycle: git worktree add when Executor sub-loop starts; git worktree remove when sub-loop ends or session exits. worktree remnants are cleaned by multimind gc.

17.4 lint/test Validation Chain

Before each auto-commit, the validation chain runs; if any step fails, auto-rollback that commit (git reset --hard to pre-commit HEAD), and reports the failure reason in the group chat:

Validation StepCommand (configurable)Failure Handling
1. Syntax checkruff check / eslint (auto-detect by project language)Rollback commit, Executor retries
2. Type checkmypy / tsc --noEmit (optional)Rollback commit, Executor retries
3. Unit testspytest -x / npm test (optional)Rollback commit, Executor retries or reports to Dispatcher
4. Custom hookProject .multimind/hooks/pre-commit.shRollback commit, report to user

Retry limit: Executor retries up to 3 times (configurable) on validation failure; after exceeding, reports to Dispatcher to decide role switch or user help. Frustration regex (§16.5) linkage: consecutive failures trigger degradation.

Fast mode: auto_commit_fast = true skips lint/test, only syntax check, suitable for exploratory phases; default off.

17.5 Checkpoint Linkage

Each auto-commit auto-creates a checkpoint anchor; the checkpoint ID is linked to the commit hash:

  • Commit success: Records (checkpoint_id, commit_hash, role, timestamp) to SQLite.
  • Rollback checkpoint: /rollback <checkpoint_id> simultaneously executes git reset --hard <commit_hash>; group-chat state and code state roll back in sync.
  • Timeline view: TUI /timeline visually shows the correspondence between commit history + checkpoint anchors + group-chat decision points.
  • Branch switching: Checkpoints are not shared across branches when switching working branches; each branch has an independent timeline.

17.6 Config Options

Add a [git] block to config.toml:

[git]
auto_commit = true              # Master switch
trigger = "step"                # step | stage | milestone | manual
work_branch_prefix = "mm/"      # Working branch prefix
protect_main = true             # Forbid direct commits to main/master
lint_before_commit = true       # Run lint before commit
test_before_commit = false      # Run test before commit (default off, time-consuming)
fast_mode = false               # Fast mode: skip lint/test
max_retry = 3                   # Validation failure retry limit
commit_message_style = "conventional"  # conventional | plain | custom
custom_hook = ".multimind/hooks/pre-commit.sh"  # Custom hook script
worktree_isolation = true       # worktree isolation for parallel Executors
gc_on_exit = true               # Clean up worktree on exit

Command-line override: multimind chat --no-auto-commit temporarily disables auto-commit; --commit-fast temporarily enables fast mode.

17.7 Security Constraints

  • Forbid committing sensitive files: .env, *.key, auth.enc etc. are forcibly excluded by .multimindignore, even if Executor tries git add, it will be intercepted.
  • force push forbidden: Auto Git never executes git push --force; pushing requires explicit /push by user.
  • Out-of-repo file protection: Only operates within git rev-parse --show-toplevel scope, no overreach.
  • Audit log: Each auto-commit is logged to the audit log (§10), including commit hash, triggering role, validation result, whether rolled back.

18 · Skill Sources & Loading Mechanism

Skills are the tool capability units that MultiMind roles can invoke. Platform skills carry IP risk — both the code layer and the prompt description layer are copyright-protected; "open use" ≠ "redistributable". This chapter defines a three-tier source strategy, IP risk isolation boundaries, and dynamic scan loading mechanism, ensuring MultiMind as an open-source project has zero infringement risk.

18.1 Core Principle: IP Risk Isolation

Problem: Directly porting platform skills (e.g., Claude Code built-in tools, Grok Build Skills), even with renamed variables, may constitute derivative work infringement. Copyright protection covers both code implementation and prompt description layers.

Principles:

  • Don't copy, don't reference, don't rename: Don't obtain others' skill source code or prompt text as a starting point.
  • Only based on public facts: API docs, RFCs, protocol specs are factual materials; self-implementing based on facts does not constitute infringement.
  • Auditable provenance: Each skill must declare source type and basis, supporting copyright audit.
  • License retention: When importing open-source skills, retain LICENSE and copyright notices as-is.

18.2 Three-Tier Source Architecture

Skills are divided into three tiers by source, with decreasing priority and increasing risk:

TierSourceIP RiskLicenseExamples
Core TierIn-houseNone (fully owned)Apache-2.0 (with project)File read/write, HTTP request, Shell execution, code search, memory ops, Git ops
Generic TierOpen-source importLow (must retain notices)MIT / Apache-2.0 / CC0Markdown rendering, JSON processing, diff tools, regex replacement
Long-tail TierLLM-generated from public docsMedium (generation process needs audit)Apache-2.0 (generated output)Specific API integration (e.g., a cloud service SDK wrapper)

Core Tier: In-House Skills

Basic capability skills written by MultiMind itself, covering core tools needed for Agent operation. Both code and prompts are original, fully owned IP, released with the project under Apache-2.0.

Skill NameCapabilityDescription
file_readRead file contentsSupports line range, encoding auto-detect
file_writeWrite fileSupports overwrite, append, SearchReplace mode
http_requestHTTP requestGET/POST/PUT/DELETE, supports streaming response
shell_execShell command executionSandboxed execution, timeout control, output capture
code_searchCodebase searchgrep/glob/semantic search
memory_opMemory operationsRead/write three-tier memory, trigger summary
git_opGit operationsCommit, rollback, worktree management (linked with §17)
session_controlSession controlcheckpoint, rollback, flatten/rebuild

Generic Tier: Open-Source Import

Generic tool skills imported from MIT / Apache-2.0 / CC0 licensed open-source projects. Must retain LICENSE files and copyright notices as-is, not delete or modify. Import flow:

  1. Confirm source project license is one of MIT / Apache-2.0 / CC0.
  2. Copy code + LICENSE file to skills/community/<name>/.
  3. Declare source_type = "open_source", license, upstream_url in skill.toml.
  4. multimind skills audit command checks license compliance.

Long-tail Tier: LLM-Generated from Public Docs

For specific API integration needs (e.g., a cloud service, a SaaS platform), the LLM self-generates skills based on their public API docs (not others' implementation code). Key constraints:

  • Input must be public docs only: API references, RFCs, official specs. Cannot input others' skill source code.
  • Generation process leaves traces: skill.toml records source_docs (referenced doc URLs) and generation_model (model used for generation).
  • Manual review: Generated skills must be manually reviewed before enabling; verified = false skills are not loaded by default.
  • Don't copy prompt descriptions: The skill's prompt description is written by the LLM based on functionality, not referencing others' prompts.

18.3 Skill Manifest Format (skill.toml)

Each skill is a directory containing a skill.toml manifest file and entry code. Manifest format:

# skill.toml — Skill manifest file
[skill]
name = "file_read"
version = "0.1.0"
description = "Read file contents, supports line range and encoding detection"
entry_point = "main.py:FileReadSkill"

[provenance]
source_type = "core"           # core | open_source | generated
license = "Apache-2.0"
upstream_url = ""              # required for open_source
source_docs = []               # required for generated: referenced public doc URLs
generation_model = ""          # required for generated: model used for generation
verified = true                # generated defaults to false, requires manual review

[capabilities]
tags = ["file", "io", "read"]  # Capability tags for routing match
requires_sandbox = false       # Whether sandbox execution is required
requires_permission = "auto"   # none | ask | auto | all
max_retries = 3

18.4 Loading Mechanism: Dynamic Scan Priority

Loading priority: dynamic scan → config file → built-in core. The three stack, not mutually exclusive.

① Dynamic Scan (Priority)

On startup, scans the following directories; any subdirectory containing skill.toml is registered as a candidate skill:

Scan PathDescriptionPriority
./.multimind/skills/Project-level skills (travel with repo)Highest
~/.multimind/skills/User-level skills (shared across projects)Medium
<package>/skills/core/Built-in core skills (ship with package)Base

Same-name skills override by priority: project-level > user-level > built-in. Scan results cache to ~/.multimind/skills.cache.json, auto-refresh on file changes.

② Config File (Supplement)

[skills] block in config.toml:

[skills]
# Extra scan directories
extra_dirs = ["/opt/shared-skills"]

# Explicitly enable/disable (overrides dynamic scan results)
enabled = ["file_read", "http_request", "code_search"]
disabled = ["shell_exec"]         # Disabled for security reasons

# Generated skills must be manually reviewed before loading
require_verified = true           # Unverified generated skills not loaded

# Model self-judgment threshold: skills with descriptions exceeding this token count are not auto-injected into context
max_description_tokens = 200

③ Model Self-Judgment (On-Demand Injection)

Does not inject all skill descriptions into role context at once, but injects on demand:

  1. On startup, scan and register all skills, but only load name + description + tags into a lightweight index.
  2. When a user task arrives, the Leader role first sees the skill index summary (name + one-sentence description + tags only).
  3. Leader judges which skills are relevant to the current task, declares need_skills = ["file_read", "code_search"].
  4. The system injects the selected skills' full prompt description + usage examples into the corresponding Executor's context.
  5. When Executor calls a skill, executes via the unified skill.execute() interface.

Benefits: avoids context bloat (100 skills' full descriptions may occupy thousands of tokens), reduces misuse risk (model only sees relevant skills), supports dynamic discovery (new skills require no code changes after joining).

18.5 Skill Execution Interface

All skills implement a unified interface, regardless of source tier:

class Skill(ABC):
    """Skill abstract base class"""
    name: str
    description: str
    tags: tuple[str, ...]
    source_type: Literal["core", "open_source", "generated"]

    @abstractmethod
    async def execute(self, args: dict) -> SkillResult:
        """Execute skill, return structured result"""
        ...

    def describe(self) -> str:
        """Return short description for model self-judgment"""
        return f"{self.name}: {self.description} [{','.join(self.tags)}]"

SkillResult contains success, output, error, metadata (token consumption, duration, etc.). Execution failure does not throw exceptions; returns success=False, caller decides whether to retry or degrade.

18.6 Skill Directory Structure

src/multimind/skills/
├── __init__.py
├── base.py              # Skill abstract base class + SkillResult
├── loader.py            # Dynamic scan + config load + model self-judgment injection
├── registry.py          # SkillRegistry (similar to ProviderRegistry)
├── audit.py             # License compliance audit
├── core/                # Core tier: in-house
│   ├── __init__.py
│   ├── file_ops.py      # file_read + file_write
│   ├── http.py          # http_request
│   ├── shell.py         # shell_exec (in sandbox)
│   ├── search.py        # code_search
│   ├── memory_ops.py    # memory_op
│   ├── git_ops.py       # git_op
│   └── session.py       # session_control
├── community/           # Generic tier: open-source import (each subdirectory contains LICENSE)
│   ├── __init__.py
│   └── README.md        # Import guidelines
└── generated/           # Long-tail tier: LLM-generated
    ├── __init__.py
    └── README.md        # Generation guidelines

# User-level skill directory
~/.multimind/skills/
├── my-custom-skill/
│   ├── skill.toml       # Manifest
│   ├── main.py          # Entry
│   └── LICENSE          # License (required for open_source)
└── another-skill/
    ├── skill.toml
    └── main.py

18.7 Audit & Security

License audit: multimind skills audit scans all loaded skills, checking:

  • Whether skills with source_type = "open_source" have a LICENSE file.
  • Whether the LICENSE is one of MIT / Apache-2.0 / CC0.
  • Whether upstream_url is accessible.
  • Whether skills with source_type = "generated" have source_docs and verified = true.

Execution sandbox: Skills with requires_sandbox = true (e.g., shell_exec) execute inside a Docker sandbox (aligned with §16.5 security system).

Permission linkage: The skill's requires_permission links with role permissions (§16.5 five permission modes) — when role permission is lower than skill requirement, user confirmation is needed before execution.

Usage tracking: Each skill execution is logged to the audit log, including skill name, caller role, parameter summary, duration, success/failure.

18.8 Relationship with Existing Systems

SystemRelationship
Provider (§4 Five Channel Types)Provider is "how to connect to AI", Skill is "what tools AI can use". Orthogonal relationship, mutually independent.
Role Orchestration (§5)Leader handles model self-judgment to select skills, Executor handles skill execution.
Memory System (§7)Skill execution results can be written to memory; memory operations themselves are a core skill.
Auto Git (§17)Git operations are a core skill; skill code changes trigger auto-commit.
MCP Protocol (§16.6)MCP tools auto-convert to typed Skills; external MCP server-exposed tools register as Skills via adapter.
Hooks Lifecycle (§16.6)Pre-execute / post-execute hooks trigger around skill execution.
Permission Modes (§16.5)Skill's requires_permission links with role permissions.

19 · SiteAdapter Site Adaptation Layer

19.1 Problem: Official Page DOM Differences

MultiMind's core vision is "chaining official pages together" — controlling official websites like DeepSeek, ChatGPT, Tongyi Qianwen, Doubao, KIMI via browser to achieve free AI multi-channel collaboration. But each website's DOM structure is completely different:

WebsiteInput MethodMode SelectionResponse Container
DeepSeekBottom rounded textareaDeep thinking / Web search (aria-pressed toggle)Markdown render container
ChatGPTcontenteditable div (not textarea)Dropdown menu (GPT-4o / Image Gen)div.markdown.prose
Tongyi QianwenBottom text box6 function tabs (image edit/deep thinking/web/web dev/image gen/video gen)div.response-content
DoubaoBottom rounded text boxQuick Q&A togglediv.message-content
KIMIBottom text boxPPT template selectiondiv.markdown-body
